summary: Spolsky argues that fixing bugs should be treated as an economic decision, not a moral imperative. He lays out a framework for calculating whether a bug fix is worth the cost by comparing the value of the fix against the cost of developer time. Using Fog Creek's own experience with FogBUGZ, he demonstrates how they calculated the ROI of a two-week bug-fixing sprint: $48,000 in developer time for $150,000 in tech support savings. He advocates for systematic bug collection, charging tech support costs back to product units to create proper incentives, and making data-driven decisions about which bugs to fix. Despite the economic framing, he concludes by erring on the side of quality, noting the hidden value of a rock-solid product.
key_points: Fixing bugs is only worthwhile when the value of the fix exceeds the cost of fixing it, and developers should make this calculation explicitly rather than treating all bug fixes as inherently good.

Companies should charge tech support costs back to product units to create the right economic incentives for fixing bugs, rather than making tech support a self-funding operation that hides the true cost of bugs.
Systematic crash reporting and treating every tech support call as evidence of a bug are essential for discovering which bugs actually matter to users.
Fog Creek calculated their bug-fixing sprint as a 3:1 ROI ($48K spent for $150K in tech support savings alone), demonstrating how to justify quality work in business terms.
Despite the ruthlessly economic framing, there is hidden value in high-quality products that is hard to quantify — employee pride, customer loyalty, and reputation.
money_quotes: Fixing bugs is only important when the value of having the bug fixed exceeds the cost of the fixing it.
By moving the economic burden of bugs onto the users themselves, you lose what limited ability you might have had to detect the damage they were causing.
It's hard to overcome the reputation of having a buggy product.
If you have to decide between fixing the bug for OS/2 guy and adding a new feature that will sell 20,000 copies of your software to General Electric, well, sorry, OS/2 guy.
When you wait long enough and have millions of users and religiously collect their stack dumps, you will find crashes in places like that and you won't believe your eyes.
I believe that there is a lot of hidden value to producing very high quality products that is not very easy to capture.
A single tech support call about the video drivers would blow away the profit you could make from twenty copies of your product.
key_insight: Bug fixing should be driven by economic analysis — calculate the cost of fixing versus the cost of not fixing — but when in doubt, err on the side of quality because reputation and pride have real if hard-to-measure value.
